prior.content_md:
**COMPETITIVE EDGE**

**Decision 1 — Scope 3 client reporting for live events accounts.**

The MIT ClimateMachine figures provide the first peer-reviewed sector-level baseline for live music logistics emissions in the US and UK. A freight forwarder who integrates these figures into client sustainability reporting — framed correctly as sector benchmarks, not per-shipment actuals — can offer context that competitors without a live events specialism are unlikely to surface proactively.

Specific decision: assess whether current per-shipment emissions reporting methodology (air freight carbon calculations, road freight emissions estimates) can be presented alongside the sector benchmark to give clients both an absolute figure and a relative position. This is a product enhancement decision, not a compliance obligation.

**Decision 2 — Modal-shift advisory for air-heavy live events clients.**

The study's directional observation that air freight has "shift to sea possible" potential creates a commercial opening. The workspace can offer structured modal-shift analysis — transit time trade-offs, advance logistics planning, equipment pre-positioning — before clients receive this framing from a venue sustainability team or a festival's ESG report. This is not a regulatory requirement; it is a commercial differentiation move while the sector is still absorbing Phase 1 findings.

Specific decision: identify three to five live events clients on regular transatlantic or transpacific air freight movements and initiate a modal-shift feasibility conversation anchored to this research.

**Decision 3 — US road freight emissions visibility.**

US trucking at 5.3% of total live music emissions is a larger share than many touring operators currently account for in their sustainability reporting. A freight forwarder can add value by surfacing road emissions data — already collected for carrier reporting purposes — in a format clients can use in their own sustainability disclosures.

Specific decision: confirm whether current operational data collection captures road freight emissions per tour leg in a format exportable to client reporting templates.

**Decision 4 — Phase 2 positioning.**

Phase 2 of the MIT ClimateMachine research is described as providing freight-specific emissions breakdowns by transport mode. When published, it will sharpen the baseline figures available to clients. A freight forwarder who has already briefed clients on Phase 1 — and framed Phase 2 as forthcoming — will be positioned to deliver Phase 2 findings as a service update rather than reacting to it when clients surface it independently.

Specific decision: set a monitoring trigger for Phase 2 publication at https://climatemachine.mit.edu/ and assign ownership for client communication when it publishes.

*Source: MIT ClimateMachine, 2025. https://climatemachine.mit.edu/*
